note_to_torami: The 5-component COI INCLUDES S_frequency by design (Paper 1 formalization v1 §3.3). High r(COI, log_freq) is EXPECTED and NOT an anti-circularity failure; it is what the formalized weights produce. The relevant question for Paper 2 is whether β(COI × cumulative) survives when log_freq is included as a separate control predictor. It DOES — all of 17b / 17c / 17m / 17n / 17o include `log_cue_freq_z` in the per-child regression alongside COI_z, so the reported β is the partial effect after log_freq is partialled out.

paper1_formalized_COI.weight_constraint: Equal weights w_i = 0.2; cross-linguistically fixed (per docs/attention_index_formalization_v1.md §2). Fixed-weight constraint is the falsifiability hinge of the framework.
paper1_formalized_COI.missing_data_rule: Listwise cue exclusion at --min_cue_count (default 5). Components default to 0.0 (or 0.5 for S_perceptual with no neighbour) when undefined for a given cue.

scaling.S_frequency_normalized: sigmoid(z-score of log token count within language).
scaling.others: Native [0,1] range; no further scaling before averaging.

paper2_SI_4component_variant_FOR_ANTICIR.purpose: Variant for SI prose anti-circularity discussion only. The headline Paper 2 analyses use the 5-component COI (caregiver_AI in the joined CSV). The 4-component variant is shown so reviewers can see how much the r drops when S_frequency_normalized is removed.

interpretation.r_5comp_vs_logfreq_is_HIGH_by_design: 5-component COI includes S_frequency_normalized = sigmoid(z(log_freq)). High r is structural, not a flaw. This was the design choice in Paper 1 (docs/attention_index_formalization_v1.md §3.3). What Paper 1 controls for is captured in the Paper 1 strategy: 07_temporal_structure.py applies `residualize()` to each temporal structure variable against log_freq, and shows that the residualized temporal structure × frequency interaction still predicts peak production. Paper 2's per-child OLS uses the same principle differently: it includes `log_cue_freq_z` as a separate control predictor alongside COI_z, partialling out the linear frequency effect from β(COI × cumulative).
interpretation.r_4comp_residual_still_~0.56_pooled: After dropping S_frequency_normalized, r(COI_4comp, log_freq) is still ~0.49 - 0.85 across languages (pooled ~0.56). This is because S_repetition (host-lemma breadth) correlates strongly with log frequency by construction (07_temporal_structure.py preamble line 10 calls this 'repetition wearing frequency's clothes'). To get a truly frequency-orthogonal COI, one must residualize each component against log_freq and re-weight, which is the framework strategy in 07-09 applied to TEMPORAL STRUCTURE, not COI itself.
interpretation.the_actual_paper2_question: Does β(COI × cumulative) survive after log_freq is included as a separate control? YES — all of 17b / 17c / 17m / 17n / 17o include log_cue_freq_z as a separate predictor, so the reported β(COI × cumulative) is the freq-partialled effect. The drop-Manchester pooled β at N=5 is +0.036 (p < .001) with both COI_z and log_cue_freq_z in the model; the partial r² attributable to COI × cumulative remains stable.

VIF_summary: VIF for COI in the per-child regression (assuming log_freq is the only collinearity source): 5-component VIF approx 2.0 - 5.6 across languages (pooled ~2.2). 4-component VIF approx 1.3 - 3.7 (pooled ~1.5). Both are WITHIN the conventional VIF<10 acceptable range, so the partial β(COI × cumulative) estimate is not inflated to the point of unreliability.
